Reference temperature from substation group analysis (Wrocław)¶

Objective: To compare Foreca temperatures with controller temperatures and check if the aggregated controller temperature can be used as a reference.¶

Total number of substations registered in CRM for Wroclaw.¶

6687

Wrocław Substations Used in Analysis¶

Only substations with coordinates were used for further spatial analysis.¶

Number of unique substations in the file: 6055

Wrocław substations in CRM: 6687
Wrocław substations with coordinates: 6055
Missing coordinates: 632

Data Cleaning and Exclusions¶

OTZ substations (Wrocław only): 356
KIONA substations (Wrocław only): 45
Total excluded (Wrocław only): 399
Filtered Wrocław pool: 6055 -> 5656 (removed 399)

Data Quality Assessment Before Analysis¶

Physical Level — Summary¶

From 5656 Wrocław substations, 12 (0.21%) recorded physically impossible temperatures (< -40°C or > 60°C).¶

These substations are marked for exclusion at the physical filtering stage.¶

APPLYING PHYSICAL FILTER TO WROCŁAW POOL
Wrocław pool before physical filter: 5656
Removed at physical level: 12
Wrocław pool after physical filter: 5644
Share removed at physical level: 0.21 %

Physical Filtering — Example¶

Example of a substation excluded due to physically impossible controller temperature values.

Logical Filtering — Summary¶

221 substations (3.92%) were excluded at the logical level due to abnormal temperature bias behaviour (fence-based and sunny anomalies).¶

The remaining clean pool contains 5423 substations.¶

LOGICAL LEVEL REPORT
Wrocław pool before logical filter: 5644
Substations removed at logical level: 221
Share removed at logical level: 3.92 %
Wrocław pool after logical filter: 5423

Controller vs Foreca Bias Analysis (Final Clean Pool)¶

The following analysis evaluates temperature bias behaviour for substations that passed all filtering stages (OTZ/KIONA removal, physical validation, and logical anomaly exclusion).

Controller vs Foreca deviation is computed using ±30 min anchor window for all substations that passed physical and logical filtering.

foreca_9 size: 24
ctrl_df size: 331516
Unique foreca timestamps: 24

Technical Summary (data coverage)¶

Active substations with matched Controller–Foreca data: 3304
Controller–Foreca comparison records used for analysis: 78902
Controller minus Foreca temperature range: -10.7 to 28.5

The following analysis evaluates temperature bias behaviour for substations that passed all filtering stages (OTZ/KIONA removal, physical validation, and logical anomaly exclusion).

Record-level distribution of all Controller - Foreca differences¶

Node-level bias ranking (mean delta per substation)¶

Distribution of mean bias across substations¶

The boxplot illustrates the distribution of mean temperature bias across substations. The median bias is slightly positive, indicating that controller readings tend to be marginally higher than Foreca estimates. Most substations exhibit moderate bias, with a small number of higher positive and negative outliers.

Logical-level filtering (IQR-based)¶

Substations with extreme mean bias values were removed using an IQR-based method.

Lower bound: -6.75°C
Upper bound: 5.25°C

True-sunny substations were also excluded. Resulting clean pool size: 5423 substations.

Anomalous substations loaded (unique): 480
WRO pool (after OTZ/KIONA): 5644
Anomalous substations inside WRO pool: 221
Rows: before -> after: 5644 -> 5423 (removed 221 )
Final clean pool size: 5423

Geographically Distributed Sample (100 Substations)¶

The map highlights 100 substations selected using a 10×10 spatial grid to ensure representative coverage across Wrocław.

Geolocated substations total: 5423
Geolocated substations with valid Controller–Foreca data: 5423
Final selected substations: 100

Representative Substations by Deviation Level¶

Three representative substations were selected from the full clean pool (after all filtering) based on aggregated mean bias across the analysed period (mean Δ):

  • Substation with bias closest to zero
  • Substation with typical (median) bias
  • Substation with largest absolute bias (strongest deviation)
['900059', '900345', '900070']

Three representative substations were selected based on aggregated mean bias across the analysed period: closest to zero, median bias, and largest absolute deviation.

substation_id mean_delta
35 900059 -1.480297e-16
197 900345 1.952271e+00
45 900070 7.968958e+00

Mean Temperature Deviation Distribution¶

To evaluate bias at the city level, we analyse:

  • distribution of mean bias across substations
  • proportion of substations within deviation ranges
  • average daily temperature curves (all substations vs grid sample)

Distribution of mean deviation¶

Note: Mean bias is calculated only for substations with valid controller–Foreca matches on the selected day (3304 out of 5423 clean substations).

Conclusion¶

The 100-substation geographically distributed sample shows an average positive bias of ~1.65°C¶

(controller vs forecast).¶

Mean bias: 1.65°C
Std deviation: 1.84°C
Conclusion:
Mean controller temperature ≈ Foreca + 1.7°C

Case Studies: Sunny and Extreme Deviation Substations¶

1. True Sunny Substation (with noon temperature hump)¶

True Sunny substations are identified using statistical detection of noon temperature hump patterns (top 5% by hump, post-drop and noon delta).

True sunny substations (with hump): 97

Best True Sunny substations: 905786

2. Not Sunny – Maximum Controller Overheating¶

Not sunny – max overheating: 906699

3. Not Sunny – Maximum Controller Undercooling¶

Not sunny – max undercooling: 904964

Anchor Window Deviation Distribution¶

The chart shows the share of substations that had at least one anchor-window extreme deviation beyond the statistical thresholds (> 5.25°C or < -6.75°C) during the analyzed day.

Total substations with anchor data: 3304

Overheated substations (max controller-foreca > 5.25°C): 2822 (85.4%)
Undercooled substations (min controller-foreca < -6.75°C): 53 (1.6%)

The high percentage of “overheated” substations (85.4%) reflects the fact that many nodes experience at least one short-term positive spike in summer conditions. Rapid daytime warming and solar exposure increase the likelihood of temporary positive deviations (Controller > Forecast).

In contrast, strongly negative spikes (undercooling) are rare (1.6%) in summer, since ambient temperatures are generally high and systemic negative deviations are less common.

Therefore, this distribution is likely characteristic of a summer scenario and reflects short-term extremes rather than systematic daily bias.
